texts.orig: ABSTRACT: This study investigates a direct air capture (DAC) process using a monoethanolamine (MEA) absorption process, further enhanced by the integration of heat pump modifications such as lean vapor compression (LVC) and stripper overhead compression (SOC) in the regeneration stage to reduce reboiler requirement. The process is modeled using Aspen Plus and incorporating the CCSI model adapted and suited for DAC environmental conditions. Technical performance was investigated through a sensitivity analysis of various environmental and process parameters, including MEA emission, air inlet temperature and humidity, and factors such as solvent loading, capture rate, gas velocity, and liquid to gas ratio. A scrubber section above the absorber was installed to mitigate MEA emission. The implementation of LVC and SOC results in a significant reduction in regeneration consumption of 25% compared to conventional MEA-based processes but the cost of capture is similar to that of the conventional process, due

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent decades, climate change has emerged as a critical issue, primarily due to the increased levels of greenhouse gases (GHG), particularly CO2, in the atmosphere. Carbon dioxide removal (CDR) solutions, which remove CO2 from the atmosphere and store it permanently, have recently gained interest in the pursuit of carbon neutrality. The advantages of CDR over point-source capture are that the capture site is decoupled from the emission site, without the need to adapt the process that emits CO 2 , giving CDR a key role in decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors such as aviation, agriculture, and shipping. Among these CDR technologies, direct air capture (DAC) is a technology that has gained significant attention in recent years as a potential solution to mitigate the impacts of climate change. 1 -5 According to the Net-Zero Scenario of the International Energy Agency, DAC could represent a capture capacity of 1 GtCO 2/yr in 2050. 6 It is a process that involves capturing CO2 directly from the atmosphere and storing it or converting it into useful products. The concept of DAC is not new, having been first introduced by Lackner et al. in the late 1990s, but recent technological advances have made it more feasible. 7,8

texts.orig: Among the different available technologies, industrial DAC process development is mainly based on absorption and adsorption. 10,15,16 However, new separation techniques based on electrochemistry and membranes are also emerging. 17 -19 There is a growing interest in the development of DAC separation techniques among academic, start-up, and industrial players. 20,21 Each method has its advantages and limitations, and ongoing research aims to improve energy requirements, overall capacity and reduce costs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Of all the industrially developed commercial-scale CO2 capture technologies, postcombustion amine-based absorption is the most mature for flue gas applications. CO 2 from the gas is chemically absorbed by an absorbent in a structured packing absorber and then recovered in a striper by temperature swing desorption while the absorbent is regenerated. In the electricity sector, these technologies have already been demonstrated on a large scale (>1 MtCO 2 /yr) at the Boundary Dam (Canada, since 2014) and Petra Nova (US, since 2017) coal-fired power plants. Among amine solvents in aqueous solution, primary amine monoethanolamine (MEA) that is also suited to low CO2 partial pressures benefits from large amount of published literature compared to other proprietary solvents and plant feedbacks. 22 -24 Unfortunately, MEA is volatile, corrosive and degrades mainly into ammonia by oxidation and under temperature. 25 -27 Due to MEA corrosion, the maximum mass fraction of MEA in aqueous solution is generally limited to 30 -35% in carbon capture applications, even with the use of inhibitors. 26 A higher regeneration temperature (>121 ° C) results in a higher desorption rate of CO2, but also leads to increased MEA degradation. Total solvent losses, due to degradation, vapor losses, and aerosols, are controlled due to the health and environmental hazards and typically range from 0.35 to 3.7 kgMEA/tCO2 in postcombustion capture pilot plants, including a reclamation process to manage the degradation products. 22,23,26 To reduce aerosol concentration downstream of the absorber, a demister can be used, while a scrubber section (or water wash section) can be used for physical absorption of MEA in water to reduce MEA vapor losses. 28 Currently, there is no global regulation outlining MEA emission limitation into the atmosphere and air quality assessment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, work has been carried out on DAC using MEA as a solvent. Barzagli et al. carried out an experimental screening of solvents for DAC, where MEA in water showed a high affinity for CO2 under DAC conditions (ambient temperature and 400 ppm-mol of CO2), with MEA in water capturing 86.9% of CO2 in air in contact with solvent during in 1 h at 25 ° C. 29 Kiani et al. simulated and optimized a DAC process using a rate-based model in Aspen Plus. 30 The process aimed to capture a specific amount of 2.3 ktCO2/yr to produce synthetic methane. A scrubber section at the absorber outlet was used to limit the emission of MEA to the atmosphere. The technical performance of the process was investigated through a sensitivity analysis on different parameters such as relative humidity, capture rate, CO 2 loading and reboiler temperature. In the base case scenario, with a 50% capture rate, a reboiler duty of 10.7 GJ/tCO2 and an electrical energy of 1.4 MW h/tCO2 was required. The absorption and scrubber sections resulted in a high capture cost (1691 $2016 /tCO2) due to the use of conventional packing and column. A hypothetical case with a low volatility solvent such as aqueous amino acids, which eliminates the need for a scrubber section and a cheaper absorber, reduces the cost to 676 $2016/tCO2. Sabatino et al. compared three optimized DAC technologies: a DAC using KOH as solvent, a DAC using MEA as solvent and a solid sorbent process. 31 All technologies have the potential to operate below 200 $/tCO 2 under favorable, yet realistic, energy and reactor costs. For DAC using MEA as solvent, solvent regeneration was found to be the largest contributor to energy demand, similar to the results of Kiani et al. 30 The reboiler duty is divided into three parts: the heat of desorption, which is the chemical energy to break down the chemical bonds formed between the CO2 and the solvent, the sensible heat, which is the thermal energy to raise the temperature of the solvent mixture without changing phase, and the stripping heat, which is the energy to remove the CO 2 from the solvent. In the conventional stripping process for flue gas capture, it has been observed that the reboiler duty is significantly affected by the latent heat of solvent evaporation, which is then condensed in the condenser, resulting in heat loss to the cooling system. 32 For flue gas capture, modifications such as lean vapor compression (LVC) and stripper overhead compression (SOC) have been shown to reduce reboiler duty for amine solvent simulations, but are not yet widely implemented in the industry. 33 -41 Scherffius et al. reported that the additional capital cost of the LVC configuration in Fluor's Econamine FG Plus typically exhibits a 1 -5 year payback period, depending on the energy cost. 42 In addition, Shell's CANSOLV cases reported by the NETL's (National Energy Technology Laboratory) study for coal and gas postcombustion capture involved a LVC modification. 43

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the knowledge and experience of the amine-based CO2 capture process, this study aims at contributing to advancements in the field of DAC technology and investigates a DAC process using the mature monoethanolamine (MEA) absorption process, further improved by integrating heat pump modifications such as lean vapor compression (LVC) and stripper overhead compression (SOC) in the regeneration stage to reduce reboiler requirements. Model selection and validation is key to providing robust simulations of the carbon capture process, especially at ambient conditions ( ∼ 15 ° C)andlowCO2 partial pressure (400 ppm-mol), which corresponds to extreme dilution. A validated rate-based DAC absorption model in Aspen Plus, adapted from a recent NETL's carbon capture model with MEA (called the CCSI model), providing robust thermody- namics, physical and chemical properties, hydrodynamics, kinetics and mass transfer models for DAC conditions, is used. 44 -48 In order to understand how the environmental conditions affect the technical performance of the MEA-DAC process, a sensitivity analysis was performed for various environmental and process parameters, including MEA emission to the atmosphere (0.1 -7.5 ppm-mol), air inlet temperature ( -5 to 35 ° C) and relative humidity (20 -100%), and also factors such as flooding capacity (25 -80%), capture rate (33 -88%), lean loading (0.2 -0.33 molCO2/molMEA) and liquid/ gas ratio (0.1 -2 kg/kg). The level of MEA emission limitation and its impact on the process, such as the size of the scrubber section, is discussed. The study then compares the process performance and capture costs of the heat pump modifications with the conventional MEA-DAC at large capture scales using Aspen Process Economic Analyzer. The processes are evaluated at large capture scales for CO2 supercritical transport and geological storage, from 0.1 to 1 MtCO2/yr. In addition, the economic assumptions such as energy price, risks and contingencies, discount rate, plant lifetime and learning rate are discussed.

texts.orig: ■ PROCESS AND MODELING
texts.text: ■ PROCESS AND MODELING

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Process Overview. A direct air capture process was modeled using MEA (hereinafter as MEA-DAC) solvent in water (30 wt % MEA). Figure 1 top shows the conventional process using an absorption column with a top-absorber scrubber section to limit MEA emission in the atmosphere, and a stripper for rich solvent regeneration after its heating, using a heat exchanger to recover sensible heat from the hot regenerated solvent exiting the stripper (economizer). Due to the low concentration of CO2 in the air, a large volume of air must be processed to capture CO2 (for example 1 m 3 of air at 15 ° C contains 0.7 gCO2). Therefore, the pressure drop of the packing in the absorption/scrubber column is a key performance indicator because it affects the work of the fan. The structured packing used in the columns is the industrial MellapakPlus 252Y (MP252Y), which provides similar separation efficiency but with lower pressure drop than Mellapak 250Y (M250Y). 49 A base case scenario is selected that is close to the well-known operating conditions of flue gas capture using MEA as a solvent, but with environmental parameters close to ambient conditions. The base case scenario combines technical, operating, and economic assumptions (see Table 1 for technical and operating assumptions; economic assumptions are discussed later). The technical and operating points are based on conditions that are in the order of magnitude to what is usually used in flue gas capture to ensure a robust application of the CCSI model, especially for solvent loading, liquid to gas ratio, flooding

texts.orig: capacity and MEA outlet emission limit. 49 -52 Moreover, the air temperature and relative humidity considered in this paper are 15 ° C and 80% respectively. Thus, the base case scenario is modeled with a liquid to gas ratio (kg/kg) of 1 in the absorber at 70% flooding capacity. Based on the typical capture rate reported by Carbon Engineering, the capture rate in the base case scenario is 75%, reducing the CO 2 concentration from 400 ppm-mol at the inlet to 100 ppm-mol at the outlet of the absorber. 10,53 The lean solvent enters the absorber ( lean inlet in Figure 1), with a lean loading factor of 0.25 molCO2/molMEA, consistent with the validity range of the model used, passes through the absorber and remains at a low loading due to the low CO2 concentration in the vapor. To increase the solvent loading, most of the rich solvent is recirculated directly to the top of the absorber via the splitter, and only a small proportion is sent to the stripper for regeneration. The solvent is regenerated at 121 ° C in the reboiler and ∼ 1.9 bar (depending on the solvent loading at the reboiler level) in the stripper. The lean solvent at the outlet of the stripper ( lean outlet in Figure 1) is regenerated at 0.18 molCO2/molMEA. The stripped CO2 at the top of the stripper is then purified to less than 40 ppm-mol water content and compressed to 150 bar and 35 ° C by a Compression and Purification Unit (CPU) for supercritical transport and storage.

The stripped CO2 at the top of the stripper is then purified to less than 40 ppm-mol water content and compressed to 150 bar and 35 ° C by a Compression and Purification Unit (CPU) for supercritical transport and storage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The configuration of this MEA-DAC has a unique feature in that solvent cooling is unnecessary. This is due to the competition in the absorber between evaporation, which cools the solvent, exothermic absorption, and heat exchange with air in the absorber. Additionally, only a small amount of stripper's solvent returns ( lean outlet at 121 ° C) at high temperatures compared to the main part of the Rich solvent that is recirculated directly at the absorber top (at about 15 ° C). Mixing the two flows results in a stable lean inlet solvent temperature that is typically close to the air temperature ( ± 5 ° C).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Preliminary simulations using conventional processes without heat pumps have confirmed that the reboiler performance is impacted by the latent heat of excess water evaporation, with approximately 70% of the reboiler duty transferred in the condenser at the top of the stripper. Therefore, heat pump integrations such as LVC or SOC modifications were evaluated for the DAC process. Figure 1 bottom left and right shows the LVC and SOC modifications in the regeneration process. The general principle of LVC is that the lean solvent at saturated liquid phase exiting the stripper is expanded, close to atmospheric pressure in our case, to produce vapor. 35 The process involves separating the liquid and vapor fractions in a flash vessel. The resulting liquid fraction is then sent to the economizer, while the vapor fraction, consisting mainly of H2O and CO2, is compressed to the stripper pressure, and then combined with a portion of the 30 ° C reflux (from the condenser) to limit the temperature to 121 ° C and is finally injected at the bottom of the stripper. The general principle of SOC is that the gas stream leaving the top of the stripper is compressed without prior water condensation. The highpressure gas stream is partially condensed at high pressure and the heat released by condensation is used to provide heat for the reboiler. The condensate is separated in a flash vessel. A new SOC stage can be added downstream if the techno-economic analysis is favorable. This study investigates the number of SOC stages in the range of 1 -3. Finally, the gaseous stream is cooled to 30 ° C, purified and compressed to 150 bar in the CPU. This configuration reduces the reboiler duty by utilizing the heat released during condensation and reduces the energy required by the compressors in the CPU. As mentioned earlier, the potential for these modifications may be limited, specifically the maximum acceptable temperature at the inlet and outlet of the compressors. 54 The maximal inlet and outlet temperatures are in the range of 100 and 175 ° C for LVC compressors and 130 and 175 ° C for SOC compressors. Section S1 of the Supporting Information gives a schematic representation of the CPU and details of the compression stages.

texts.orig: The base case scenario considers a European DAC located near the sea, with an air reference temperature of 15 ° C and a relative humidity (RH) of 80% as atmospheric conditions. The LVC modification, which is the most advanced heat pump modification for reducing reboiler duty, has been chosen as a reference for this work and named hereinafter as the 'LVCcase'. The number of compressor stages of compression was also investigated regarding the SOC modification. Conventional case with a conventional stripping process allowed for a comparison with heat pump modifications.

texts.orig: Process Model. The MEA-DAC process was simulated using an Aspen Plus V14 rate-based MEA model. Our MEA model is an adaptation for direct air capture application of the MEA Steady State Model (called CCSI model ). 44 -48 The CCSI model was developed in Aspen Plus by the NETL's project called Carbon Capture Simulation for Industry Impact (CCSI 2 ). This model was previously developed for an aqueous monoethanolamine solvent-based CO2 capture system using structured MellapakPlus 252Y packing with a high interfacial area. It was rigorously validated against a pilot system and industrial flue gas at the National Carbon Capture Center . 47,48

texts.orig: The thermodynamic model is based on the ELECNRTL method. This method uses the Redlich -Kwong equation of state to calculate the vapor phase fugacity coefficients and the Electrolyte Nonrandom Two Liquid model to calculate the activity coefficients in the liquid phase. CO2, N2, O2, Ar and MEAare considered as Henry components. Stand-alone models were developed to determine the viscosity, density, and surface tension of the MEA -CO2 -H2O system. The models were based on independent regressions of experimental values. 44 Additionally, models were developed to determine liquid -vapor equilibrium (LVE), heat of absorption, and heat capacity through simultaneous regression of experimental data. 45 The heat transfer coefficients were calculated using the Chilton and Colburn analogy. Additional information about the CCSI model is available on the dedicated Web site and in the referenced articles. 51

texts.orig: A rate-based calculation method is used to model the absorber, scrubber and stripper. The Mixed f low model is considered in the columns. As no reactions occur in the vapor phase, only a film resistance is considered. The liquid film is divided into 20 spatially distributed points, with more points closer to the interface, to facilitate the occurrence of reactions in the absorption and scrubber sections. Liquid film reactions are considered in the stripper. Section S2 of the Supporting Information summarizes the models used in absorption, scrubber and stripper sections.

texts.orig: A sensitivity analysis was then conducted on the LVC-case to investigate its technical performance. The analysis considered various environmental and process parameters, such as MEA emission, air inlet temperature and humidity, and factors like capacity of flooding, capture rate, lean loading, and liquid-to-gas ratio (L/G ratio). The analysis was carried out using a 'one-at-atime' method, where parameters are tested one at a time rather than multiple factors at the same time, allowing the impact of individual parameters on system performance to be assessed. The ranges of tested parameters are shown in Table 3, and the reference points are the same as those of the base case scenario, except when specified.

texts.orig: capture and compression for a further transport and permanent geological storage of 100 ktCO2/yr, 500 ktCO2/yr, and 1 MtCO2/yr. To investigate the operational scalability of the capture system, two approaches using learning rate and change of scale are combined. The first is based on parallel components when their maximum size is reached due to technical and logistical challenges, taking into account a learning rate on costs. The second is based on a change of scale until the maximum component size is reached. 67,68 Given the maturity of CO2 capture processes using MEA and its application to a novel DAC process, a learning rate of 5% has been selected. 69 -71 Typically, a 5%learning rate implies that the cost of capture decreases by 5% each time production is doubled. In the Aspen Plus simulations, the CO2 production (tCO2/yr) is based on the maximum production achievable with a 12.8 m diameter absorber (cross sectional area of around 130 m 2 ), which in our cases represents approximately 5.5 tCO2/yr. This diameter value is based on the typical maximum column diameter possible for carbon capture and to properly distribute gas and liquid phases. 26,64 On this basis, the first approach for the absorption/scrubber column is to use parallel components when their maximum size is reached. To increase CO2 capacity, the number of identical absorbers with a diameter of 12.8 m should be increased, which would lower the cost of capture by applying a learning rate. In the second approach, for components that can be mutualized and increased in size to large scale without technological limitations, such as LVC, SOC or CPU, their capital cost is directly estimated at the capacity being considered. The LVC, SOC, and CPU costs are estimated directly at scale by APEA. The total field cost (TFC) is calculated by adding these costs. 65 Section S4 of the Supporting Information provides details on the equipment mapping in APEA and materials for the cost estimation. Other costs are evaluated using multiplying factors, as described in the Table 4. The base case scenario assumes a plant lifetime of 20 years and a discount rate of 8%. For plant operation costs, an annual maintenance cost of 3% of the TPC is assumed. Labor costs are evaluated based on 100 full-time jobs for a production of 1 MtCO2/yr, with linear extrapolation for other production scales. 72 Electricity and heat prices are assumed to be 70 €/MW h and 10 €/GJ, respectively.

texts.orig: Initially, the simulation is run in the base case scenario (see Table 1) to observe the performances of the columns at 15 ° C. In the absorption section, air flows through structured packing in counter-current with the solvent, capturing CO2. The molar fraction of CO 2 decreases from 400 to 100 ppm-mol along the absorption section (Figure 3 top left). The solvent temperature is slightly lower at the bottom of the absorber due to H2O evaporation and heat exchange with the air flow, which has a greater cooling effect than the heating effect of the absorption reaction heat. The temperature of the inlet air at the absorber bottom is slightly higher than the ambient air temperature because of the air compression by the centrifugal fan before it enters the column. The rich solvent exiting the absorber is split into two parts, and a small fraction is sent to the stripper where the CO2 is desorbed from the rich solvent at 1.91 bar and T reboiler = 121 ° C. The concentration of CO2 is enriched at the top of the stripper (Figure 3 top right) before being sent to the condenser.

texts.orig: The productivity of LVC-case is in the low range of the range estimated by Balasubramaniam et al. for DAC using solid adsorbents, which ranges from 0.41 to 6.3 kgCO2/(h · madsorbent 3 ). 77 The productivity is estimated considering only the volume of the active material, structured packing volume for absorption here and adsorbent volume for adsorption. The productivity does not consider other elements such as pumps, compressors, tower structure, etc. The significant amount of water lost through evaporation could be a bottleneck in situations of water scarcity. The amount of MEA lost through evaporation is much higher than the losses estimated for evaporation and degradation in a postcombustion capture pilot plant at the Niederaussem lignite-fired power plant (0.35 kgMEA/tCO2). 22 The simulation did not evaluate degradation losses in long-term operation with high O 2 content in air and thermal cycling. Furthermore, the pressure drop per unit length (Pa/m) in the column is over 10 times higher than the value reported by Carbon Engineering ( ∼ 15 Pa/m) in their DAC that uses potassium hydroxide solvent and cross-current flow with PVCpacking. 10 This high pressure drop in the column leads to a high electric fan work of about 2 MW h/tCO2, which accounts for 54% of the total energy requirements. More than half of the reboiler duty is transferred in the condenser (5.3 GJ/tCO 2 ) at the top of the stripper.

texts.orig: Air Inlet Temperature and Relative Humidity. Figure 4 displays the results for air inlet temperature and relative humidity. To represent weather conditions at high and low temperatures, relative humidity was set to 70% while air temperature sensitivity ranged from 0 to 35 ° C. Productivity remains constant between 5 and 25 ° C but decreases outside of this range (Figure 4 top left). At high temperatures, the decrease in productivity is attributed to a shift in the liquid -vapor equilibrium toward the desorption of CO 2 . At low temperatures, reduced kinetics lead to decreased productivity. When the air temperature drops below zero, the absorber temperature also drops below 0 ° C (not shown in figure because negative temperature in column is not desirable). Although mixing 30% wt MEA with H2O lowers the freezing point below 0 ° C(around -16 ° C) in the absorption section, the scrubber section, which contains only a small amount of MEA in H 2 O, can be affected by temperatures close to 0 ° C. 78 To avoid negative temperatures in the absorption/scrubber column, a significant amount of energy is required (Figure 4 top right) to heat the solvent. Heating the solvent with the heat of condensation from the stripper condenser alone is insufficient when temperature is below -1.5 ° C because the required energy surpasses the stripper condenser energy (5.3 GJ/tCO2) and the energy of condensation in the condensers of the CPU are not sufficient (<0.1 GJ/tCO2 per condenser). Depending on the cost of low-quality heat, such as waste heat, and the frequency of negative air temperatures, it may be more cost-effective to stop plant operations during cold days. The impact of air temperature on solvent evaporation at high temperatures is significant, especially for temperatures above 25 ° C for MEA evaporation (Figure 4 middle left).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At air temperature of 35 ° C, the evaporation of H2Oincreases as the relative humidity decreases (Figure 4 middle right). Lower relative humidity leads to less MEA evaporation because the cooling effect of H2O evaporation reduces the solvent temperature. A similar trend, to a lesser extent, is observed at an air temperature of 15 ° C. Productivity is impacted by relative humidity at 35 ° C (Figure 4 bottom), but not at 15 ° C. This is due to the higher evaporation of water at high temperatures, which has a greater impact on solvent temperature. As previously mentioned, high temperatures decrease productivity. Therefore, high relative humidity reduces water evaporation, resulting in a higher temperature in the column and lower productivity.

texts.orig: Monoethanolamine Emission. MEA is volatile and hazardous to both human health and the environment, highlighting a need for assessment across diverse conditions. Therefore, emissions are typically controlled to ensure that they are not released into the atmosphere at concentrations above the limits set by regulations. Currently, there is no global regulation for MEA emission limitation. For example, the CO2 Technology Center in Mongstad (TCM), Norway, has a flue gas capture demonstrator that restricts gas outlet to 6 ppm-mol and allows a maximum flow of 2.8 t/yr for primary amines like MEA. 79 As per French labor laws, the exposure limit for workers is 1 ppm-mol during an 8 h workday. 50

texts.orig: Liquid-to-Gas Ratio. The liquid-to-gas ratio (L/G ratio) is analyzed between 0.1 and 2 kg/kg. The height of the absorption section is adjusted to maintain a 75% capture rate. As shown in Figure 8 top left, at lower L/G ratios, the absorption section is higher to maintain the 75% capture rate, resulting in lower productivity. In addition, a lower L/G ratio results in a higher CO2 flow in contact with the solvent and a higher loading of the rich solvent, resulting in a lower reboiler duty for regeneration (Figure 8 top right). As can be seen in Figure 8 bottom left and right, at a lower L/G ratio, the gas velocity is higher, resulting in a taller column and a higher pressure drop and fan work. However, the pump work required to recirculate the solvent is less due to a higher rich loading. Determining the optimum L/G ratio requires careful consideration because of the opposite effects of L/G ratio on reboiler duty, productivity, fan work and pump work.

As shown in Figure 8 top left, at lower L/G ratios, the absorption section is higher to maintain the 75% capture rate, resulting in lower productivity. In addition, a lower L/G ratio results in a higher CO2 flow in contact with the solvent and a higher loading of the rich solvent, resulting in a lower reboiler duty for regeneration (Figure 8 top right). As can be seen in Figure 8 bottom left and right, at a lower L/G ratio, the gas velocity is higher, resulting in a taller column and a higher pressure drop and fan work. However, the pump work required to recirculate the solvent is less due to a higher rich loading. Determining the optimum L/G ratio requires careful consideration because of the opposite effects of L/G ratio on reboiler duty, productivity, fan work and pump work.

texts.orig: Flooding Capacity. The flooding capacity of the absorber is typically 70%. The flooding capacity is varied from 25% to 80% by varying the air and solvent flows while keeping other parameters constant (the L/G ratio is kept constant at 1 kg/kg). Varying the air flow results in changes in gas velocity (Figure 9 right). As shown in the Figure 9 left, even at a 75% capture rate, a higher flooding capacity (i.e., higher gas velocity) results in a higher absorber height, but also higher productivity due to the increased air flow (i.e., CO2 flow). The pressure drop in the column is affected by the combination of high gas velocity and high absorber height at high flooding capacity (Figure 9 right). A trade-off between the effect of productivity and pressure drop on the cost of capture should be made to find the optimum flooding capacity.

texts.orig: To determine the scrubber height at the controlling sizing point (i.e., in warm weather under envelope operating conditions), the methodology involves simulating at 35 ° C, followed by simulating under reference conditions at 15 ° C. The results and comparisons of the three outlet emission concentrations are shown in Table 6. As mentioned in the sensitivity analysis, the scrubber height increases as the limit becomes more stringent, resulting in lower productivity because of the need for more packing volume. In addition, the scrubber height affects the pressure drop of the column and the fan work, influencing the operating costs. A higher height affects the capital cost due to the need of larger packing and column structure. The capital cost (CAPEX cost) has a greater influence on the cost of capture than the operating costs (electricity cost). However, it is important to note that MEA losses, which include evaporation and degradation, also affect the cost of capture. It is worth noting that only evaporation is affected by scrubber height, while degradation is estimated at 1.6 kgMEA/tCO2 for each case based. 26 Based on the assumption of 2 €/kgMEA, the LVC-case (6 ppm-mol) is the cheaper option as it has less stringent constraints. 81

texts.orig: LVC, SOC and Conventional Configurations Comparison. The purpose of this section is to assess the cost and performance of capturing 500 ktCO2/yr of CO2 in five cases: LVC-case, SOC1-case (1 compressor stage), SOC2-case (2 compressor stages), SOC3-case (3 compressor stages), and Conv-case for a capture rate of 75% (Table 1). The main results of the key performance indicators are presented in Table 7. The processes vary only in the regeneration, as explained in the 'Process and Modeling' section. Therefore, the indicators linked with absorption, such as H2O and MEA losses, pressure drop, and fan and pump work, are almost the same for each case. However, the LVC and SOC cases result in a significant reduction of the reboiler duty compared to the Conv-case. The LVC-case offers a reboiler duty gain of 29%, while the SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case offer even higher gains of 10%, 57% and 72%, respectively. However, the energy gain is balanced by a CAPEX penalty due to LVC and SOC modifications in the regeneration process. The electric work of the compressors in the SOC or LVCmodifications partially offsets the reboiler duty, making the SOC and LVC cases more electricity-intensive than the conventional process. Additionally, compressing the vapor at the top of the stripper in the SOC modification reduces the compression need of the CPU, with electricity reduction in CPU of 13%, 22% and 33%, respectively, for SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case.
texts.text: LVC, SOC and Conventional Configurations Comparison. The purpose of this section is to assess the cost and performance of capturing 500 ktCO2/yr of CO2 in five cases: LVC-case, SOC1-case (1 compressor stage), SOC2-case (2 compressor stages), SOC3-case (3 compressor stages), and Conv-case for a capture rate of 75% (Table 1). The main results of the key performance indicators are presented in Table 7. The processes vary only in the regeneration, as explained in the 'Process and Modeling' section. Therefore, the indicators linked with absorption, such as H2O and MEA losses, pressure drop, and fan and pump work, are almost the same for each case. The LVC-case offers a reboiler duty gain of 29%, while the SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case offer even higher gains of 10%, 57% and 72%, respectively. Additionally, compressing the vapor at the top of the stripper in the SOC modification reduces the compression need of the CPU, with electricity reduction in CPU of 13%, 22% and 33%, respectively, for SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case.

texts.orig: Figure 10 displays the distribution of CAPEX and OPEX costs for the LVC-case and shows that the MEA-DAC process is CAPEX intensive. More specifically, the absorption section corresponds to over 50% of the capital cost, while the scrubber section accounts for almost 25%. The energy cost of the fan is linked to the pressure drop in the absorption/scrubber sections. With an electric cost of 70 €/MW h, the cost amounts to approximately 140 €/tCO2. Comparatively, the cost of the stripper is relatively small, representing only about 2% of the capital cost due to the high recirculation of the solvent.

texts.orig: To reduce the capture cost, it is suggested to first reduce the capital cost by developing and testing under long-term operation new absorption/scrubber packing and columns with cheaper and lighter materials, such as plastic packing, e.g., hydrophilic modified polypropylene packing. 87 Additionally, packings with lower pressure drop and optimized geometry can lead to a reduction in fan work. The packing used in this study, MP252Y, has a 45 ° inclination geometry. Generally, packings with 45 ° inclination geometry exhibit better mass transfer efficiency compared to those with 60 ° inclination geometry, but they also have higher pressure drop. 88 Moreover, further experimental research is needed to fully validate the model predictions under varied environmental conditions and long-term operation.

texts.orig: Figure 12 shows that sources of electricity production with low carbon footprint exhibit high positive carbon efficiencies higher than 50% even for a DAC with high electricity requirements, while gas source of electricity exhibits very low carbon efficiency and even negative carbon efficiency when electricity requirements exceed 2 MW h/tCO2 . The high energy requirements and electricity carbon footprints greatly influence the carbon efficiency. The reduction of energy demand and the use of low-carbon and dispatchable energy, e.g., nuclear, geothermal and hydropower, provide a good opportunity to achieve high carbon efficiency, close to 100%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the base case scenario of the LVC-case, MEA and H2O losses were high with 1.5 kgMEA/tCO2 and 4.9 tH2O/tCO2. A strict limitation on MEA emission reduces process productivity and increases the cost of capture by 8.2% for the 0.1 ppm-mol limit compared to the 6 ppm-mol limit. At high air temperature and low relative humidity, H2O loss was higher than 10 tH2O/ tCO2. At air temperatures below 0 ° C, the solvent must be heated by an external heat source to prevent negative temperatures in the absorption/scrubber column.
texts.text: In the base case scenario of the LVC-case, MEA and H2O losses were high with 1.5 kgMEA/tCO2 and 4.9 tH2O/tCO2. A strict limitation on MEA emission reduces process productivity and increases the cost of capture by 8.2% for the 0.1 ppm-mol limit compared to the 6 ppm-mol limit. At high air temperature and low relative humidity, H2O loss was higher than 10 tH2O/ tCO2. At air temperatures below 0 ° C, the solvent must be heated by an external heat source to prevent negative temperatures in the absorption/scrubber column.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Heat pump integrations allow a large reduction of more than 25% in regeneration energy requirements compared to conventional stripping, but the electrical and thermal energy requirements of the DAC process are still high with about 2.7 MW h/ tCO2 and 8.2 GJ/tCO2 for the LVC-case, respectively. These high energy requirements require low carbon intensity energy to achieve high carbon efficiency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ahigh cost of capture (>1000 € 2022/tCO2) has been evaluated for all process configurations at initial development. In the reference LVC-case (1 ppm-mol MEA emission concentration limit and 500 ktCO2/yr), the cost of capture is 1139 € 2022/tCO2. Economies of scale allow a 15% reduction in capture cost from a capture scale of 100 ktCO2/yr to 1 MtCO2/yr.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MEA-DAC process is capitalistic with 61% of the cost attributed to the capital cost and 39% to the operating cost for the LVC-case. The absorption/scrubber column accounts for 75% of the capital cost. Further research on cheaper packing, with lower pressure drop, compatible with MEA solvent for the absorption and scrubber sections, and cheaper column architectures/structures are needed to reduce the cost of capture and fan work, thus helping the process to ensure a better efficiency.
